text: Leaves change color in autumn because shorter days and cooler temperatures signal trees to stop producing chlorophyll—the pigment that drives photosynthesis and gives leaves their green color. As chlorophyll breaks down, previously masked yellow, orange, and red pigments are revealed. SUNY ESF +1The vibrant fall foliage is driven by three main pigments that dictate the exact colors you see: Chlorophyll (Green): Abundant during spring and summer, it absorbs sunlight to convert into energy. It masks all other pigments until the tree begins to prepare for winter.Carotenoids (Yellow & Orange): These pigments are always present in the leaf but are hidden by the overwhelming green of chlorophyll. When chlorophyll fades, yellow and orange colors shine through. They are the same pigments found in carrots and corn.Anthocyanins (Red & Purple): Unlike carotenoids, these are actively produced in autumn. They are created when sugars get trapped in the leaf as it prepares to drop. Smithsonian Institution +6How Weather Impacts ColorThe brightness and intensity of fall colors are heavily influenced by autumn weather conditions: NASA Earthdata (.gov)Sunny & Cool: Bright sunny days followed by cool, crisp (but not freezing) nights create the most vibrant reds by trapping sugars in the leaves.Wet & Warm: A mild, overcast, or excessively wet autumn will cause colors to fade and turn brown much faster. Woodland Trust +1To learn more about the biological processes happening in different tree species, check out the Smithsonian Institution's Guide or the USDA Forest Service explanation of fall foliage.
